food_subgroup: Stir-fried vegetable dishes with legumes and fungi

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists specifically for Beijing-style sauteed assorted vegetables with roast bran and peanuts. FOODON_03311737 (stir-fried vegetable dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (stir-fried vegetables) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for Beijing-style mixed vegetable stir-fry with roast bran, mushrooms, and peanuts.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept exists for this specific prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Beijing-style sauteed assorted vegetables is approximately 200 g, served as a shared side dish or individual accompaniment to rice.

nutrient_source.title: Stir-fried mixed vegetables with peanuts — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central entries for roast wheat gluten (kao fu), shiitake mushrooms, wood ear mushrooms, peanuts, lily buds, and tofu skin, weighted by typical recipe proportions
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods and SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Beijing-style sauteed assorted vegetables (京味儿素什锦). Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for the principal ingredients: roast wheat gluten / seitan (kao fu, ~30%), shiitake mushrooms (~20%), wood ear mushrooms (~10%), peanuts (~15%), dried lily buds (~10%), tofu skin (~10%), and cooking oil/soy sauce (~5%). Proportions are based on standard Beijing home-cooking recipes. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — mixed vegetable dishes and legume-containing stir-fries
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Beijing-style sauteed assorted vegetables. GI estimated at 32 based on analogous mixed vegetable stir-fry dishes with legumes and fungi from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021). The dish is dominated by low-GI ingredients: wheat gluten (kao fu, GI ~30–35), mushrooms (GI ~10–15), peanuts (GI ~14), and tofu skin (GI ~15). Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (32 × 9.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 6.1 g, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A 200 g serving provides approximately 640 mg sodium, representing about 28% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-soy-sauce preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high dietary fibre from mushrooms, wheat gluten, and peanuts
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 32 and a glycemic load of approximately 6.1 per serving, this dish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed as part of a balanced meal; the peanuts and dietary fibre further attenuate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Unsaturated fat profile from peanuts and plant-based ingredients with zero cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish is cholesterol-free and provides predominantly unsaturated fats from peanuts and vegetable oil; however, the moderate sodium content warrants attention for patients with hypertension-related cardiovascular risk, and portion control is advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: 京味儿素什锦 (Jīng wèir sù shíjǐn), translated as Beijing-style Sauteed Assorted Vegetables, is a traditional Northern Chinese vegetarian cold or lightly stir-fried dish originating from Beijing cuisine. The dish typically combines roast wheat gluten (kao fu / 烤麸), shiitake mushrooms, wood ear mushrooms (黑木耳), dried lily buds (金针菜), tofu skin (腐竹), and peanuts, all seasoned with soy sauce, sesame oil, sugar, and Shaoxing rice wine. The ingredients are individually prepared — mushrooms are soaked and blanched, peanuts are roasted, and wheat gluten is braised — before being combined and tossed in the seasoning. The result is a savoury, slightly sweet, and umami-rich dish with a variety of textures ranging from chewy (wheat gluten, tofu skin) to crunchy (peanuts, wood ear mushrooms) and tender (lily buds, shiitake). Nutritionally, the dish is a good source of plant-based protein (approximately 6.8 g per 100 g), dietary fibre (2.8 g per 100 g), and iron (1.8 mg per 100 g). The peanuts contribute healthy unsaturated fats. The dish is naturally cholesterol-free and suitable for vegetarian and vegan diets. Its low glycemic index (estimated GI ~32) makes it appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The primary clinical concern is sodium from soy sauce seasoning (~320 mg per 100 g), which is relevant for hypertensive patients. In Singapore, this dish is served at Beijing-style Chinese restaurants as a cold appetiser or shared side dish, often priced around SGD 10–11 per portion.

lunch or dinner accompaniment to steamed rice

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific Beijing-style dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for the principal ingredients. GI is estimated by analogy from the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable mixed vegetable and legume stir-fry dishes. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy) for the principal ingredients of this Beijing-style dish; no single approved-source entry exists for 京味儿素什锦 as a prepared dish. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a plant-based dish with no hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central ingredient data. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as all ingredients are plant-based. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g (-1 sentinel not used; value 2.8 estimated from peanut and vegetable oil contribution). 6. All -1 values in per_100g represent nutrients not reliably reported across the composite ingredient sources in USDA FoodData Central at the level of precision required; specifically: no -1 values remain as all fields have been estimated or confirmed. 7. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database for mixed vegetable stir-fries with legumes and fungi; no direct GI measurement for this dish was found. 8. Sodium estimate of 320 mg per 100 g reflects typical soy-sauce-seasoned Beijing vegetable dishes; actual sodium may vary significantly by restaurant preparation. 9. The dish contains gluten from roast wheat bran (kao fu) and soy from soy sauce; not suitable for coeliac or soy-allergic individuals. 10. Peanut allergen is present.
